text: The surgeon introduces the full circle pulpotomizer uterine manipulator, which holds the anterior and posterior leaf of the cervix. The uterus is pushed towards the opposite shoulder as the right round ligament is dissected 4 cm lateral to the uterus, followed by the fallopian tube and ovarian ligament. The broad ligament is dissected 2.5 cm on the right side after taking adnexal structures. The uterus is then pushed towards the left shoulder, and the left round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovarian ligament are dissected. The broad ligament is dissected 2.5 cm on the left side. The uterus is retroverted and pushed to the five o'clock position. The left hand stretches the peritoneum of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, while the right hand uses harmonic to open it. The assistant rotates the uterus from five to seven o'clock position, separating the anterior peritoneum. The uterus is at the seven o'clock position, and the right anterior leaf of the broad ligament is separated.

The surgeon separates the bladder from the vagina using blunt dissection, revealing the criss-cross pattern of vaginal vessels and shiny vaginal fascia. The bladder is moved laterally to prevent injury to the ureter. The bladder pillars are separated on both sides. Following the separation of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, the posterior peritoneum is opened, with the uterus positioned from ten o'clock to two o'clock to facilitate the separation.

The surgeon coagulates the uterine artery using a ligature device, applying it two to three times on the left side before moving to the right side. Saline and betadine are used between the jaws to minimize smoke and maintain temperature control. After proper coagulation, the uterine artery is divided, revealing the tip of the uterine structure.

The surgeon performs colpotomy using a harmonic device, starting from the left side and progressing in a clockwise fashion. The assistant rotates the colpotomizer to facilitate the procedure. The anterior and posterior colpotomy are completed, ensuring the vaginal part of the uterosacral ligament remains intact. The uterine artery on the left side is secured, and the eschar from the ligature is cleaned with betadine. The uterus is then extracted through the vagina, followed by suction irrigation to check for bleeding.

The surgeon uses stone holding forceps to push a needle through the vagina for suturing. Continuous suturing begins at the right utero-sacral ligament, proceeding to close the posterior leaf of the vagina. Care is taken to avoid the bladder during anterior suturing. The suturing involves two layers, moving from right to left and then returning left to right, with interlocking stitches at the corner. The assistant holds the suture tail to facilitate the process, culminating in a surgeon's knot for vault closure.
The surgeon completes the vault closure with a double wrap suture technique, followed by two alternating wraps to approximate the corners. Scissors are used to cut the suture, finalizing the closure.
